coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots anchoring on copper-vacancy-rich Cu nanowires/Cu foam as the cathode in microbial fuel cells: Role of C-S-Cu active site

coredata.dc:description: Oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalysts have been considered as one of the key components in microbial fuel cells (MFCs). Heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from biomass have attracted wide attention due to their rich functional groups, excellent properties, and environmental friendliness. Herein, orange-peels-derived N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) are in-situ anchored on copper-vacancy-rich Cu nanowires/Cu foam (V-Cu NWs/CF), obtaining the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs, to catalyze ORR in MFCs. The interaction between N, S-CQDs and V-Cu NWs/CF from the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs is bridged by the C-S-Cu bond, which is demonstrated to be the active site towards ORR and plays an important role in promoting electron transfer by in-situ Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy characterizations. In MFCs, the maximum power density (924.5 ± 32.5 mW·m−2) of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs is 1.34 times that of Pt/C (686.5 ± 28.0 mW·m−2), and its long-term stability also outperforms the Pt/C. This study provides inspiration for synthesis of efficient ORR electrocatalysts with metal-ligand active sites creating by heteroatom-doped CQDs and cationic-metal-vacancy-rich materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalysts have been considered as one of the key components in microbial fuel cells (MFCs). Heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from biomass have attracted wide attention due to their rich functional groups, excellent properties, and environmental friendliness. Herein, orange-peels-derived N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) are in-situ anchored on copper-vacancy-rich Cu nanowires/Cu foam (V-Cu NWs/CF), obtaining the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs, to catalyze ORR in MFCs. The interaction between N, S-CQDs and V-Cu NWs/CF from the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs is bridged by the C-S-Cu bond, which is demonstrated to be the active site towards ORR and plays an important role in promoting electron transfer by in-situ Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy characterizations. In MFCs, the maximum power density (924.5 ± 32.5 mW·m−2) of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs is 1.34 times that of Pt/C (686.5 ± 28.0 mW·m−2), and its long-term stability also outperforms the Pt/C. This study provides inspiration for synthesis of efficient ORR electrocatalysts with metal-ligand active sites creating by heteroatom-doped CQDs and cationic-metal-vacancy-rich materials.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial fuel cells (MFCs), as an environmental-friendly biotechnology which is able to convert chemical energy storing in organics in wastewaters into electricity, have been applied in the fields of electricity production, self-powered biosensors, and wastewater treatment in the last decade (Ahmadpour et al., 2020; Qiu et al., 2020). In the MFC, the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) can significantly affect the MFC performance. Although platinum (Pt) has been recognized as the most active electrocatalyst for ORR because of its superior catalytic properties, its high price and easy of being poisoned hinder its practical application (Zhang et al., 2020c). Therefore, development of cost-effective electrocatalysts with high catalytic activity remains a challenge.

To construct efficient electron transfer active center, strategies of regulating electronic structure and coordination environment by doping heteroatoms or construction of metal-cation defects could be adopted (Liu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021a). Metal-cation vacancies could lead to redistribution of electron density, thus accelerating charge transfer, and providing active sites for ORR (Liu et al., 2018; Sanaei and Fatemi, 2021). Li et al. (Li et al., 2019b) demonstrated that bimetallic defects (manganese and cobalt) could improve the ORR activity by adjusting the electrical conductivity and optimizing the adsorption and activation of oxygen (O2) at the active site. Recently, transition metal-derived vacancy materials have attracted increasing attention due to their excellent properties in optical and electrochemical fields, such as photoelectrochemical (PEC) (Yang et al., 2021), water splitting (Wang et al., 2019), methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) (Guo et al., 2020), etc. Besides, our previous study also showed that in-situ growth of electrocatalysts with Cu vacancies on substrate material could improve electrical conductivity and increase effective active sites (Lan et al., 2021). However, due to the challenges of their higher formation energies than those of anion vacancies (Liu et al., 2018), information on adopting metal-cation-defect engineering in MFCs is very limited so far. More importantly, how the metal vacancies create ORR active sites is still unclear. Therefore, it is urgently required to study the influence and mechanism of cation vacancy defects on electrocatalytic ORR activity.

In addition, transition metal‑carbon composites with low toxicity and excellent catalytic activity have also attracted much attention in the field of cathode modification, because they could regulate the coupling between metal active sites M-N-C (M is a transition metal, N is a heteroatom) and catalytic activity (Ding et al., 2019; Jing et al., 2018; Wan et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as the low-cost, non-toxic, and high-conductivity carbon materials, have good biocompatibility and unique photoelectric properties, thus often being used as sensors and catalysts in biomedical and optoelectronic fields (Tian et al., 2021). It was reported that N-doped CQDs iron/iron oxide (Fe@Fe2O3) nanoparticles showed efficient and stable electricity-producing performance (Farahmand Habibi et al., 2021b). Another study demonstrated that doping of heteroatoms can optimize electronic structure and produce abundant of defect edges, thus promoting electron transfer (Guo et al., 2021b). Generally, efficient CQDs could be derived from cost-effective, widely-available biomass with desired structures and abundant of functional groups. Accordingly, excellent performances could be achieved since large number of active sites could be created for efficient ORR (Shaik et al., 2020). Previous researches indicated that orange-peels-derived carbon materials exhibited desired ORR catalytic activity and stability in fuel cells (Dhelipan et al., 2017; Lam et al., 2016). Therefore, excellent ORR catalytic activity of orange-peels-derived CQDs compositing with other materials should be expected. Nevertheless, when CQDs were compounded with other materials, the active sites may be covered due to the aggregation of CQDs, causing deteriorated performance (Aniskevich et al., 2018). Besides, recent researches showed that better atomic coordination could be obtained on Cu-vacancy-rich substrate (Zhang et al., 2021b), because defect sites have unique electronic properties for forming new coordination structures to achieve superior performance (Li et al., 2019b). Therefore, introducing Cu vacancies into transition metal sulfide, and then anchoring biomass-derived CQDs on Cu-vacancy-rich materials with coordination unsaturated was supposed to be conducive to uniform dispersion of CQDs and generation of more efficient catalytic active centers.

Accordingly, the objective of this work is to in-situ anchor biomass-derived N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) on copper-defects-rich Cu nanowires (V-Cu NWs/CF) as the ORR electrocatalyst (N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs). Specifically, orange peels with rich network structure, low cost, abundant in source, were selected as the biomass for N, S co-doped CQDs synthesis. Copper foam (CF) was used as the substrate material for Cu vacancy creation. The copper sulfide layer was firstly synthesized on copper substrate to create lattice defects; then, N, S-CQDs were anchored on the above material through electrodepositing process to obtain N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (Fig. 1 ). The physico-chemical properties of the as-prepared materials such as crystal structure, morphology, hydrophobicity, and surface composition, etc. were systematically characterized; besides, electrochemical properties were also determined. More importantly, the ORR catalytic mechanism between Cu-vacancy-rich catalyst and N, S co-doped CQDs was revealed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and in-situ Raman characterizations. Ultimately, their performance and durability in MFCs were studied.

Oranges peels waste were obtained from daily life. Copper foam (CF, thickness: 2.0 mm, pores per linear inch: 5–120 ppi), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), thiourea, acetone, ethanol were purchased from Rhodes Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanning, China). All chemicals were reagent grade and used as received without further purification.

The cleaned orange peels were dried at 70 °C in an electric oven and triturated after cooling down. Then, 10.0 g of dried orange peels were soaked in H2SO4 solution (1 M) under ultrasonication for 2 h, and neutralized in ice bath of NaOH solution (1 M) for 15 min and washed with deionized water. The source of C precursor powder was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and N, S-CQDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: Modified synthesis of CQDs/N, S-CQDs solution via a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment was as follows (Karaman, 2021): Firstly, 0.08 g of orange peels powder and 0.16 g of thiourea with a mass ratio of 1:2 were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water; then, the mixture was subjected to ultrasound for 1 h at room temperature. After that, the resulting solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and heated at 200 for 12 h. Subsequently, the yellow-brown solution was filtrated through 0.22 μm membrane to remove large particles, and the as-obtained N, S-CQDs solution were stored in the refrigerator. The CQDs solution was obtained without adding thiourea using the above procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of copper sulfide coated Cu foam (Cu1.81S/CF)
ce:section.ce:para: The CF was cleaned with acetone and undergone ultrasonic treatment in HCl solution (1 M) for 15 min. After that, the CF was immersed in thiourea solution (2 M) and ultrasonication for 5 min. The material with black surface was denoted as Cu1.81S/CF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of copper-vacancy-rich Cu nanowires/Cu foam (V-Cu NWs/CF)
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu1.81S/CF was electrochemically treated in NaHCO3 solution (0.5 M) under the potential of −1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for 30 min to obtain more defects (Zhang et al., 2021b). The V-Cu NWs/CF was obtained by electrochemical reduction and washed with ethanol and deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs anchoring/Cu2O decorated Cu foam (CQDs/Cu2O-CF and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-CF), CQDs anchoring /Cu2O decorated Cu nanowire (CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs)
ce:section.ce:para: Consist with most QDs-based electrocatalysts, the as-obtained materials were prepared by electrodeposition method to obtain good conductivity (Aniskevich et al., 2018). The CQDs and N, S-CQDs solution were deposited on pure CF or V-Cu NWs/CF using two-electrode electrochemical method under the potential of 6.0 V (vs. Pt electrode, 0.5 M NaHCO3) for 20 min, respectively. The as-obtained materials were washed with deionized water. According to XRD results, they were labeled as CQDs/ Cu2O-CF, N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-CF, CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs, and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs, respectively. The calculations of mass loading were provided in Table S1.

To determine the effect of electrochemical deposition time on materials performance, different electrochemical deposition times (10 min, 30 min) of CQDs/N, S-CQDs solution were carried out. The as-prepared materials were denoted as CQDs/Cu2O-10-CF, CQDs/Cu2O-30-CF, N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-10-Cu NWs, and N, S-CQDs/ Cu2O-30-Cu NWs, respectively.
Four mg Pt/C was dispersed in the mixture of 30 μL Nafion and 1 mL isopropyl alcohol via ultrasonication for 30 min; then, the catalyst ink was coated onto the CF using a brush and dried overnight.

A series of techniques were adopted to characterize the physico-chemical properties of the as-prepared materials, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy disperse X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscope (TEM), high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Characterization instruments and operating parameters were detailly described in Supplementary Materials. The in-situ Raman experiments were recorded at room temperature using a Raman spectrometer (in Via Reflex, Britain) with a 532 nm laser and source spectral range from 100 to 1200 cm−1. The inoperando Raman characterization was performed using a custom-made spectro-electrochemical cell. The as-prepared materials, Pt wire, and saturated calomel electrode were used as the working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode, respectively. Inoperando Raman spectrum of the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs was collected with an acquisition time of 20 s under I-t measurement from 0 V to −1.3 V vs. SCE (O2-saturated PBS, 50 mM). A confocal Raman microscope coupled with a 90-degree angled Olympus 50 × objective was used for all tests. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) assay was performed to verify the existence of vacancy by Bruker A300e (Brooke). In addition, the UV-vis adsorption spectra was analyzed using an UV-2501PC (SHIMADZU Japan) spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQDs and N, S-CQDs were tested with a spectrofluoro-photometer (ZLX-PL-I, Beijing Zhuoli Hanguang Instrument Co., Ltd.).

All electrochemical measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (IviumStat.h, Holland) using a three-electrode system. The as-prepared electrodes were served as the working electrode, the graphite rod as the counter electrode, and the SCE as the reference electrode. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 50 mM) was used as the electrolyte. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted within the potential range of 0.2 V to −1.0 V with a scan rate of 5 mV·s−1. Tafel curve was recorded via sweeping from the overpotential range (η) of 0–100 mV with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at an open circuit potential (OCP) from 105 to 10−2 Hz, and the charge transfer resistance (Rct) was obtained by ZSimpWin 3.30 software (Echem, US). Current-time (I-t) test was conducted at −0.163 V (vs. SCE).

The double-chamber MFC reactor was the same as our previously described (Lan et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2020). The anode and cathode were connected using titanium wire. All of the MFCs were operated with a 1000 Ω external resistor. The graphite carbon brush with biofilm acclimated on it was adopted as the anode, and the above-prepared electrodes were used as the cathode, and O2 (provided by aeration, with the aeration flow of 2.0 L·min−1, and PBS as the catholyte) was the electron acceptor. The ingredients of the medium was the same as previously described (Lan et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2020). The output voltage was continuously recorded using a data acquisition system (model 2700, Keithley Instruments, Inc., Cleveland, OH). MFCs were operated parallelly at room temperature, and the medium was refreshed when the output voltage dropped to lower than 0.1 V. Polarization curve was obtained as previously described (Lan et al., 2021, Yan et al., 2020).

The XRD patterns were shown in Fig. 2a. The main peaks obtained matched well with that of the standard Cu (PDF No. 04–0836), corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes, respectively. The weak peaks of 37.506°, 46.084°, and 48.540° belonged to the copper sulfide coated Cu foam (PDF No. 41–0959) in Cu1.81S/CF. Parts of the sulfide layer was electrochemically reduced to metal copper to create more defects, resulting in weaker copper sulfide peak covered by the main peak of CF and disappeared in V-Cu NWs/CF. Furthermore, the main characteristic peak of Cu2O (111) at 37.091° (PDF No. 34–1354) could be resulted from the reaction with orange peels or oxidation of Cu in air (Li et al., 2019a). Studies have shown that the presence of Cu2O can improve the electrochemical performance (Waqas et al., 2020). More importantly, the magnified region in Fig. 2b showed that with the introduction of heteroatoms of N and S, the characteristic peak strength of Cu decreased and shifted to a higher angle, which could be due to the change of lattice constant caused by co-doping of N and S (Farahmand Habibi et al., 2021a), or change caused by CQDs anchoring on V-Cu NWs /CF containing defects (Wang et al., 2021).

As presented in FTIR spectra (Fig. 2c), abundant of functional groups banded at 3450 cm−1, 3100 cm−1–3250 cm−1, ~2330 cm−1, 1456 cm−1, 1650 cm−1, 1400–1550 cm−1, ~1375 cm−1, ~1170 cm−1, and ~510 cm−1 were observed, which could be assigned to O-H, C-H/N-H, -C=NH+-, C=O, C=C, C-N/C-O, C-C, C-S/C-S-C, and Cu-O, respectively (Naveenkumar et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018). The diversity of functional groups could provide rich active sites for ORR activity. In addition, the band at ~620 cm−1 was attributed to the existence of Cu-S/Cu-S-C (Guo et al., 2021a; Li et al., 2017). These peaks from N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-CF were significantly enhanced compared with those without N, S co-doping (CQDs/ Cu2O/-Cu NWs and Cu2O/CQDs/CF). Therefore, it was speculated that the Cu-S/Cu-S-C bonds could be the active sites for promoting ORR.

Raman spectra were performed to analyze the structural defects of carbon materials. As showed in Fig. 2b and Fig. S1, the D-band (1340 cm−1–1400 cm−1) and G-band (1560 cm−1–1700 cm−1) represented sp3 defects and sp2 hybridization of carbon, respectively. The materials with N, S co-doped CQDs were deconvoluted into five characteristic peaks at ~1320 cm−1, ~1380 cm−1, ~1470 cm−1, 1550 cm−1, and 1590 cm−1, which could be assigned to the defect bands and small ordered fused benzene rings (D), methyl group and amorphous carbon (V1), semicircle ring breathing (V), aromatics with 3–5 rings (G1), and highly ordered sp2 graphitic carbon (G), respectively (Cao et al., 2020; Wan et al., 2019). Obviously, those N, S co-doped CQDs materials showed a wider D band as compared with CQDs materials, and the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs had the highest intensity ratio (ID/IG = 1.20), indicating the N, S co-doped was the main contributor to create more C defects and edges of disordered structure, thus changing charge/spin distribution and facilitating electron transfer (Bai et al., 2021). In addition, the intensity ratios of CQDs/Cu2O-CF and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-CF were higher than those of CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs, indicating there could be a synergistic effect between Cu vacancies and the CQDs, and subsequent EPR tests also confirmed this phenomenon.

Hydrophobicity of the electrode is an important parameter for ORR reaction, which has been viewed as one of the major factors influence the efficiency of fuel cells (Wang et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2019). Fig. 2d-g shows that the average contact angle values of all materials were higher than 90°. Specially, the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs catalyst exhibited the highest contact angle of 127.72°. It was reported that the presence of Cu2O may also improve the surface hydrophobicity of materials (Qu et al., 2019). The superhydrophobic electrode with particularly underwater Wenzel-Cassie coexistent state (UWC-state) electrode can greatly improve the efficiency of gas consumption reaction in ORR and affect mass transfer and electron transfer behavior at the liquid/gas/solid interface (Wang et al., 2017). More importantly, hydrophobic electrodes could accelerate the contact between reactants and the active sites, thus improving the catalytic efficiency. This could be one of the reasons for the excellent performance of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs.

Fig. S2a-b presents the morphology of Cu1.81S /CF and V-Cu NWs/CF. The morphology of reduced V-Cu NWs/CF changed from needle-like nanowires to porous loofah nanowires with anisotropic structure. As shown in Fig. S2c-d, Cu2O/CQDs-CF showed the dodecahedral shape of Cu2O with aggregation and size difference, while N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-CF showed cube shape of Cu2O. However, CQDs were hardly observed, possibly due to the small size and low loading amount. Interestingly, after electrodeposition, the Cu2O dodecahedron/cube self-assembles were twined by V-Cu NWs/CF porous loofah nanowires, and the sizes were reduced (Fig. S2e-f). The morphology of CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs basically maintained the shape of loofah nanowires. Different morphology, transverse size, thickness, and growth direction were found when Cu2O was grown at high voltages (Fig. S2c-d). The EDS mappings (Fig. S3) showed that the amount of C from N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs was higher than that of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-CF, and the direct loading of CQDs on CF resulted in a limited content of C, suggesting that the porous V-Cu NWs/CF loofah nanowires with rich defects can anchor more CQDs.

The morphology and structure of the as-prepared CQDs and N, S-CQDs aqueous solutions were characterized by transmission electronic microscopy (TEM). Fig. 3a shows that the N, S-CQDs were uniformly distributed, with the average size
d ¯
= 2.03 nm (inset of Fig. 3a). Fig. 3b displays that the lattice spacings of N, S-CQDs at 0.21 nm, could be indexed to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon. Moreover, compared with N, S-CQDs, the particle size of CQDs was larger and there was agglomeration phenomenon (Fig. S4), indicating that N and S played an important role in regulating morphology and size.

The HRTEM image of Cu1.81S/CF illustrated the loading of the sulfide layer successfully (Fig. 3c). In Fig. 3d, lattice discontinuities, including interstitial atoms (point defects) and intragranular dislocations (plane defects), could be observed from V-Cu NWs/CF, which could provide a highly active surface for anchoring of CQDs (Guo et al., 2020). According to particle size distribution diagrams of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (Fig. 3e), the N, S-CQDs were uniformly distributed. The crystal lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was corresponded to the (100) graphite plane (inset of Fig. 3f), consistent with previously reported literature (Dong et al., 2013). Furthermore, the N, S-CQDs deposited on N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs were more evenly distributed than CQDs deposited on Cu2O/CQDs-Cu NWs under different electrodeposition times (Fig. S5).

To gain deeper insight into defects, EPR assay was obtained (Fig. 4 ). The signal at g = 2.07 ± 0.01 provided strong evidence for the existence of defective Cu (Kang et al., 2020; Lan et al., 2021). This observation supported the aforementioned HRTEM result. Obviously, compared with Cu1.81S/CF, the V-Cu NWs/CF exhibited stronger signal intensity, indicating that the V-Cu NWs/CF contained more copper defects (inset in Fig. 4), which could provide a favorable environment for anchoring N, S-CQDs. Interestingly, the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs exhibited the highest peak intensity among four materials; and the peaks strengths of the CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs were significantly stronger than that of the defective Cu precursor. This result showed that the concentration of Cu vacancies increased after N, S-CQDs loading, which was likely due to the introduction of negative charges of S and N into C lattice, leading to the C lattice being disordered, thereby generating more active edges, so as to improve the Cu activity of coordination (Zhang et al., 2020b). This result further confirmed the coupling effect between CQDs and Cu-vacancy-rich metal materials.

High-resolution XPS spectra were conducted to analyze chemical compositions and surface elemental valence states of V-Cu NWs/CF, CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs, and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs. As shown in Fig. 5a, the elemental components of the three samples included Cu, S, N, C, O. In the Cu 2 p spectrum (Fig. 5b), the peaks at 930.2 eV and 950.1 eV could be assigned to Cu 3p3/2 and Cu 3p1/2 of Cu0/Cu+, while the binding energies at 932.6 eV and 952.7 eV could be ascribed to Cu 3p3/2 and Cu 3p1/2 of Cu2+ (Zhang et al., 2020a). Meanwhile, the Cu0 and Cu+ species were further distinguished by Auger Cu LMM spectrum (Fig. S6), and the peak at ~570 eV was consistent with the typical value of Cu+ (Liu et al., 2017). Additionally, the ones located at 941.0 eV and 960.8 eV could be attributed to the satellite peaks of Cu 3p3/2 and Cu 3p1/2, which corresponded to the Cu-S bond accompanied by S 2 p spectrum (Zhao et al., 2017). For the high-resolution spectrum of S 2 p (Fig. 5c), in contrast to V-Cu NWs/CF, CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs could be deconvoluted into four peaks: the major peaks at 159.8 eV and 160.9 eV were attributed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of C-S-Cu bond, and peaks at 162.4 eV and 165.5 eV were assigned to C-S-C (Ding et al., 2019; Jing et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2020). The C-S-C bond has been proved to be able to change charge spin density and collaborate with doped N to promote ORR activity (Yan et al., 2020). In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 5d), the peaks at 282.6 eV, 283.9 eV, and 286.9 eV were possibly attributed to C-C/C=C bonds, C-S/C-S-Cu bond, and C-N bond, respectively (Ding et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020). More significantly, compared with V-Cu NWs/CF, the Cu peaks from CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs were slightly redshifted about 0.3 eV, while the S peaks showed a positive shift about 0.7 eV, and the C peaks were blueshifted about 0.2 eV, indicating that there was a stronger interaction between V-Cu NWs/CF and CQDs in the CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs via the bridge of C-S-Cu (Ding et al., 2019; Ning et al., 2021). The C-S-Cu bond was possibly indispensable for the active site to improve electrochemical performance (Zhu et al., 2020). In addition, the metal-S-C bond could bring about denser local electronic states to form more reactive sites (Jing et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2020). Fig. 5e shows the binding energies of the N 1 s, and four peaks were corresponded to N-H (396.7 eV), pyridinic N (397.7 eV), pyrrolic N (399.3 eV), and graphitic N (401.5 eV), respectively (Yang et al., 2018). It is well known that pyridinic N and graphitic N play an important role in ORR. Specifically, pyridinic N is able to weaken the O-O bond and accelerated the ORR process, while graphitic N can reduce the mass transfer resistance, thus improving the electrochemical performance (Zhong et al., 2019). For O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5f), the peak at 529.9 eV was originated from the lattice oxygen in the Cu2O, and the peak at 530.1 eV was assigned to the chemisorbed oxygen (Liu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2020a).

Furthermore, Fig. 5g-i and Table S2 showed the preliminary quantitative analysis of chemical bonds using the fitting peak area. Compared with V-Cu NWs/CF, a certain amount of C, N, and S decreased in CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (Table S3). Yet, C content of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs was higher than that of CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs, indicating that there were more C active sites on N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs. More importantly, the content of C-S-Cu bond was the highest in S 2 p, and the content of C-S-Cu bond was higher than that of C-N bond in C 1 s, suggesting that C-S-Cu bond could play a crucial role in ORR reaction. These XPS results further demonstrated the successful synthesis of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and the strong synergistic coupling of C-S-Cu bond between copper-vacancy-rich Cu NWs/CF and CQDs.

The in-situ Raman test was performed to further explore the mechanism of activity sites in the ORR process. As displayed in Fig. 6 , the Raman band at ~126 cm−1 may belong to the characteristic of Cu-O. Two peaks located at about 430 cm−1 and 750 cm−1 were assigned to the stretch modes of Cu-S/Cu-S-C and C-S/C-S-C, respectively (Andrew et al., 1994; Baldwin et al., 1996; Noh et al., 2007). Interestingly, the peak intensity of the Cu-S/Cu-S-C stretching started to increase with the increase of applied potential, and reached the maximum at −0.6 V, and then gradually decreased and almost disappeared at −1.1 V, which was a favorable evidence for active site of Cu-S-C for ORR. Another peak of C-S/C-S-C basically disappeared at extremely negative potential of −1.0 V. According to relevant literature, thiourea was easily converted to dimethyl sulfide in water, and the C-S bond in these thioether compounds can be selectively broken or even completely broken under certain conditions (such as ORR reaction and photochemical reaction) (Hauptmann and Walter, 1962; Vedejs and Perry, 1984), which was speculated to be the reason for the disappearance of Cu-S-C and C-S/C-S-C bond. Briefly, strong interaction existed between metal vacancies and biomass carbon in the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs via the bridge of C-S-Cu active site.

Furthermore, the impact of N, S co-doping on electron transfer was investigated using UV–vis DRS and PL measurements. As shown in Fig. S7, the UV–vis DRS result proved that N, S was co-doped into CQDs successfully; and PL result further indicated that N and S co-doped can effectively promote the separation of photoelectrons from vacancies and improve electron transfer. Detailed discussion was provided in Supplementary Materials.

The LSV results were displayed in Fig. 7a. and Fig. S8a. The maximum current density of the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (88.33 × 10−3 mA·cm−3) was 1.45 times of the Pt/C (54.35 × 10−3 mA·cm−2), 1.92 times of the CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (62.58 × 10−3 mA·cm−2), 1.82 times of the N, S-CQDs/ Cu2O-CF (66.95 × 10−3 mA·cm−2), 3.52 times of the CQDs/Cu2O-CF (43.03 × 10−3 mA·cm−2), respectively. The highest current density observed from N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs indicated a greater number of active sites available per unit of surface area, which could be mainly due to the rich of C-S-Cu active site to boost electrocatalytic activity. Besides, the doping of N, S could synergistically optimize the local electronic structure, thus promoting chemical absorption of oxygen (Karaman, 2021). Additionally, the V-Cu NWs/CF exhibited the maximum current density of 57.03 × 10−3 mA·cm−2, which was 2.56 times of the Cu1.81S/CF (44.32 × 10−3 mA·cm−2). The high current density of V-Cu NWs/CF could be attributed to abundant Cu vacancies which exposed more active sites, thereby accelerating the electron transfer.

The Tafel curves were conducted to evaluate the electron dynamics coefficient of ORR for the electrocatalysts (Fig. 7b and Fig. S8b), and the calculated exchange current densities (i 0) were listed in Table S4. The higher i 0 means a lower kinetic coefficient of forward ORR reaction (Ym et al., 2019). The i 0 of different cathodes followed an descending order: N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (238.616 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-30-Cu NWs (171.830 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-10-Cu NWs (114.419 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-CF (110.662 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), Cu2O/CQDs-Cu NWs (91.180 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), CQDs/Cu2O-10-Cu NWs (88.511 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), V-Cu NWs/CF (78.722 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), Pt/C (73.080 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), CQDs/Cu2O-30-Cu NWs(70.794 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), Cu1.81S/CF(63.694 × 10−5 mA·cm−2), CQDs/Cu2O-CF (62.820 × 10−5 mA·cm−2) and CF(55.988 × 10−5 mA·cm−2). The N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs showed the highest slope among all materials, likely attributing to the construction of highly active center of C-S-Cu, rich copper vacancy, and high content of N, S-CQDs, which improved electron transfer.

The EIS was conducted to reveal the resistance and electron transfer of the materials, and results were displayed in Fig. 7c and Fig. S8c. The fitted charge transfer resistances (Rct) of different cathodes followed the order (Table S5): N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs(11.78 Ω) < N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-10-Cu NWs (12.80 Ω) < CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (13.54 Ω) < CQDs/Cu2O-10-Cu NWs (13.91 Ω) < N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-30-Cu NWs (15.93 Ω) < CQDs/Cu2O-30-Cu NWs (23.99 Ω) < N, S-CQDs/ Cu2O-CF (24.21 Ω) < CQDs/ Cu2O-CF (26.09 Ω) < Pt/C (29.36 Ω) < CF (34.23 Ω) < V-Cu NWs/CF (111.10 Ω) < Cu1.81S/CF (263.90 Ω). Compared with the Cu1.81S/CF, the remarkably decreased Rct for the Cu-vacancy-rich V-Cu NWs/CF showed that the Cu vacancy could promote electron transfer (Chen et al., 2021). Accordingly, the materials with CQDs anchoring on Cu-vacancy-rich V-Cu NWs/CF exhibited lower Rct than those with CQDs directly modified on CF. Besides, the Rct of materials with N, S co-doped CQDs were lower than those with pure CQDs prepared under similar deposition condition (Table S4), which suggested that the synergistic effect between N, S co-doped CQDs and Cu vacancy could improve charge transfer on electrode surface. Moreover, the interaction between CQDs and Cu2O promoted the rapid electron mobility, which may also be the reason for the improved electrical conductivity (Li et al., 2015).

The electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) at different scan rates of all materials were presented in Fig. S9. As listed in Table S6, the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs exhibited the highest ECSA (322.857 mF·cm−2), followed by CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (291.428 mF·cm−2) and Pt/C (182.857 mF·cm−2). The porous loofah nanowires with anisotropic structure and cubic Cu2O allowed exposure of more electron adsorption and transfer channels by self-assembly, resulting in more active sites to facilitate the reaction. This was consistent with the result of FESEM. In short, due to the rich of C-S-Cu active sites, N, S co-doping, rich of Cu defects, and high specific surface area, the conductivity of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs was improved, resulting in the most desired onset potential, half-wave potential, exchange current density, Tafel, Rct, and ECSA (Fig. 7e and Table S6).

Durability is one of the key parameters for industrial application of the electrocatalysts. Fig. 7d presents the stability of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs and Pt/C cathodes in PBS solution. The N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs exhibited 53.3% decay of current after 173,860 s, while Pt/C decreased by 76.2% after 122,140 s, demonstrating that the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs should be a desired electrocatalyst with acceptable durability. Moreover, the XRD pattern of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs after working was almost the same with the initial one (Fig. S10).

Fig. 8 presents the performance of MFCs constructed with different cathodes. Consistent with previously reported literature, the cathodic performance was the main factor affecting the power output of MFCs (Fig. 8a). As shown in Fig. 8b, obviously, the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs cathode exhibited the best performance in the MFC, with the maximum power density (MPD) of 924.5 ± 32.5 mW·m−2, which was 1.34 times of that of Pt/C cathode (686.5 ± 28.0 mW·m− 2. MFCs with other cathodes showed lower MPDs as follows: N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-CF (482.0 ± 26.1 mW·m−2), CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs (472.7 ± 18.1 mW·m−2), V-Cu NWs/CF (163.7 ± 9.4 mW·m−2), CQDs/Cu2O-CF (108.3 ± 11.5 mW·m−2), and Cu1.81S/CF (103.7 ± 9.7 mW·m−2), respectively. Moreover, the maximum current density of the MFC with N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs cathode (3.352 A·m−2) was 1.28 times of that of Pt/C cathode (2.617 A·m−2), and 1.57 times of that of CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs cathode (2.136 A·m−2). Besides, compared to the other cathode materials, the cell voltage of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs reduced rather slowly, suggesting the minimum polarization, possibly owing to the quick charge transfer kinetics and excellent conductivity of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs.

In addition, the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs cathode also exhibited better durability than Pt/C cathode in MFCs (Fig. 8c,d). After running for 30 d, the MPD from the MFC with the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs cathode decreased from 924.5 ± 32.5 mW·m−2 to 638.7 ± 9.0 mW·m−2 (decreased by 30.9%); while the MPD of Pt/C decreased from 686.5 ± 28.0 mW·m−2 to 328.8 ± 28.0 mW·m−2 (decreased by 52.1%) (Fig. 8d), suggesting superior long-term durability and stability of the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs cathode. Compared with some related carbon materials (Table S8), the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs also exhibited comparable or even better power yield and long-term stability. For instance, the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs in our study outperformed the BGQDs/MOF-t (703.55 mW·m−2) (Yan et al., 2022); and the MPD of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs was 3.53 times that of WRC-700 (262 mW·m−2) (Zhong et al., 2019), 2.91 times that of AC-KOH-1 h (317.7 ± 0.4 mW·m−2) (Jiao et al., 2020), and 1.81 times that of HC-900 (511.5 ± 25.6 mW·m−2) (Ye et al., 2019). Moreover, the cost of N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs was even lower (0.06 $·g−1) than some related materials, such as GO-Zn/Co (0.064 $·g−1) (Yang et al., 2019) and MFC-EGC1-10 (0.049 $·g−1) (Lu et al., 2017), and V2O5-NR/rGO (0.024 $·g−1) (Noori et al., 2017). These comparisons suggested that the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs catalyst should be a promising ORR catalyst for practical application from the perspectives of performance, durability, and cost.

In summary, in-situ anchoring of orange-peels-derived N, S-CQDs onto Cu-vacancy-rich substrate material as the efficient ORR electrocatalyst has been successfully synthesized (the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs) and applied in the MFC. The interaction between the Cu-vacancy-rich material and orange-peels-derived N, S-CQDs in the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs was bridged by the highly active site of C-S-Cu bond, which was beneficial to improve the electrocatalytic activity towards ORR. The newly designed N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs exhibited desired electronic structure, favorable hydrophobicity, excellent electrochemical performance, and acceptable stability. The maximum power density from the MFC with the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs cathode was much higher than that of Pt/C (924.5 ± 32.5 mW·m−2 vs 686.5 ± 28.0 mW·m−2), and the stability of the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs cathode in the MFC also outperformed the Pt/C cathode. The superior performance, desired long-term stability, and low cost make the N, S-CQDs/Cu2O-Cu NWs a promising cathode material for practical application in MFCs.
